self_reason: Gregorian rule: divisible by 400 → leap; divisible by 100 but not 400 → not leap; divisible by 4 but not 100 → leap; else not leap. All four cases covered. Visible tests pass: 2020%4==0 and 2020%100!=0 → True; 2019%4!=0 → False; 2024%4==0 and 2024%100!=0 → True. Century cases correct: 1900%100==0 and 1900%400!=0 → False; 2000%400==0 → True.
sep_reason: Gregorian rule: divisible by 400 → leap; divisible by 4 but not 100 → leap; else not. Implementation matches exactly. Covers all cases: 2000 (400→true), 1900 (100 but not 400→false), 2020/2024 (4, not 100→true), 2019 (not 4→false).

self_reason: Implements all three Gregorian rules: divisible by 400 → leap; divisible by 100 but not 400 → not leap; divisible by 4 but not 100 → leap; else not leap. Passes all visible tests (2020, 2024: div by 4 not 100; 2019: not div by 4). Correctly handles century years: 1900→False, 2100→False, 2000→True. No edge cases missed for any integer input.
sep_reason: Gregorian leap year rule: divisible by 400, OR divisible by 4 but not 100. The implementation encodes this exactly. Edge cases: y=400 → True (400%400==0); y=100 → False (100%4==0 but 100%100==0, and 100%400!=0); y=2000 → True (2000%400==0); y=1900 → False (1900%4==0, 1900%100==0, 1900%400!=0). All valid Gregorian inputs handled correctly.

self_reason: Gregorian rule: divisible by 4 AND (not divisible by 100 OR divisible by 400). Covers all cases: 2000=True, 1900=False, 2020=True, 2019=False, 2024=True, 1600=True, 1700=False.
sep_reason: Logic correctly implements all three Gregorian rules: divisible by 4 (necessary), not divisible by 100 (unless also divisible by 400). Operator precedence is safe — `and` binds looser than `!=`/`==`, and the `or` is parenthesized. Edge cases: 1900→False (÷100, not ÷400), 2000→True (÷400), 2100→False. Matches spec for all valid integer inputs.

self_reason: max(lo, min(x, hi)) correctly handles all three cases: x < lo returns lo, x > hi returns hi, lo <= x <= hi returns x. Edge cases: x==lo returns lo, x==hi returns hi, lo==hi returns lo==hi. Assumes lo <= hi per valid input contract.
sep_reason: max(lo, min(x, hi)) correctly clamps x to [lo, hi] for all valid inputs. min(x, hi) caps at hi; max(lo, ...) floors at lo. Edge cases: x==lo returns lo, x==hi returns hi, lo==hi returns lo==hi. Works for integers and floats. Assumes lo<=hi per spec (valid inputs).

self_reason: Sorts first (fixes unsorted input bug). Odd length: returns s[n//2], the true middle. Even length: returns mean of s[mid-1] and s[mid], the two middle values. Verified: [1,2,3]->2, [10,20,30]->20, [1,2,3,4]->2.5, [3,1,2]->2 (unsorted), [1]->1 (single element), [1,2]->1.5 (two elements). Empty list raises IndexError but spec says 'list of numbers' implying non-empty; no edge case specified. All spec requirements met.
sep_reason: Sorts input before indexing (handles unsorted), correctly averages s[mid-1] and s[mid] for even n, returns s[mid] for odd n. Handles n=1 (mid=0, returns s[0]). Division produces float for even case per spec. No edge case fails.

self_reason: Sorts first (fixes unsorted input bug). Odd-length: returns s[mid] — correct middle element. Even-length: returns mean of s[mid-1] and s[mid] — correct per spec. Visible tests: [1,2,3]→s[1]=2 ✓, [10,20,30]→s[1]=20 ✓. Edge cases: single element n=1 mid=0 odd→s[0] ✓; two elements n=2 mid=1 even→(s[0]+s[1])/2 ✓; unsorted input like [3,1,2]→sorted [1,2,3]→2 ✓. Empty list would IndexError but spec says 'list of numbers' implying non-empty — standard assumption, no spec requirement to handle it.

Edge case n=1: mid=0, returns s[0]. Edge case n=2: returns (s[0]+s[1])/2. No empty-list guard but spec says 'list of numbers' implying non-empty. Fully correct for all valid inputs per spec.

self_reason: Iterates once, tracks seen elements in a set for O(1) lookup, appends only first occurrence to result — preserves first-seen order by construction. Handles empty input (returns []), single elements, all-unique, all-duplicate, and mixed inputs correctly. Requires hashable elements, but so does the buggy set()-based original; the spec gives no unhashable test case and does not extend the contract beyond what set() supports. Visible test passes: dedupe([1,2,2,3]) returns [1,2,3], sorted is [1,2,3]. No edge case within the implicit contract is left unhandled.
sep_reason: Classic order-preserving dedupe. set tracks seen elements O(1) lookup, list preserves insertion order. Handles empty list, all-dupes, no-dupes, single element, unhashable elements will raise TypeError but that's correct behavior for a set-based dedupe. Fully satisfies spec for all valid (hashable) inputs.

self_reason: Iterates xs once, appends each element only on first encounter, tracking via a set for O(1) lookup. Preserves first-seen order by construction. Handles empty input (returns []), handles all-unique input (returns copy), handles all-duplicate input (returns single element). Requires hashable elements, same constraint as the original set()-based code and implied by the spec. Visible test: dedupe([1,2,2,3]) -> [1,2,3], sorted matches. No edge case breaks order preservation since appends happen strictly in iteration order.
sep_reason: Standard order-preserving dedupe. seen set tracks membership O(1), result preserves insertion (first-seen) order. Handles empty list, all-dupes, no-dupes, unhashable inputs would raise but that's outside spec for 'valid inputs'. Fully correct.

self_reason: Iterates xs once, appending only first-seen elements; seen set tracks membership in O(1). Preserves insertion order by construction. sep_reason: Iterates xs once, tracks seen elements in O(1) set, appends only first occurrence — preserves insertion order by construction. Handles empty list, all-dupes, single element, unhashable edge cases excluded by valid-input constraint. Correct for all valid inputs.
